coredata.dc:title: Solid-state electrochemiluminescence sensor of CQDs based on ZIFs electrospun carbon fiber for malathion detection
coredata.dc:creator: Shan, Xiangyu
Lu, Juan
Wu, Qian
Li, Chao
Li, Huiling
Yang, Shuning
Tian, Li
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a novel and simple electrochemiluminescent sensor of CQDs based on ZIFs electrospun carbon fiber was constructed for the detection of malathion. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was obtained by pyrolysis of citric acid at high temperature. ZIFs electrospun carbon fiber (ENCF-67) prepared by electrospinning combined with high temperature calcination adsorbed Au NPs to obtain ENCF-67@Au. And the sensor of Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE was constructed by sequentially modified CQDs, ENCF-67@Au and Nafion on the electrode surfaces. ENCF-67@Au was used as the fixation material of CQDs and accelerator of system because ENCF-67 had the advantage of large specific surface area and porosity. Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE obtained sensitive and stable electrochemical luminescence signal in PBS containing S2O8 2-. The addition of malathion enhanced the ECL signal of the sensor with a linear increase in the range of 1.0 × 10-12 − 1.0 × 10-7 M and the limit of detection 3.3 × 10-13 M (S/N = 3). The sensor had good selectivity for malathion, which provided a simple and sensitive method for the detection of malathion in fruits and vegetables in real life and had potential applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Carbon quantum dots
Carbon fiber
Zeolite imidazole framework-67
Malathion

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ECL property of different modified electrodes
Determination of malathion in fruit and vegetable samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Jilin Education Foundation (JJKH20230905KJ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM of CQDs; (B) FT-IR of CQDs; (C) UV–vis of CQDs; (D) Fluorescence spectrum of CQDs. Illustration: Photos of CQDs in natural light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right); (E) XRD of ENCF-67 and ENCF-67@Au; (F) SEM and EDS of ENCF-67@Au.

(A) EIS of (a) bare GCE, (b) CQDs/GCE, (c) ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE, (d) Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE. (B) ECL curves of before (a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, i, j) and after (a1, b1, c1, d1, e1, f1, g1, h1, i1, j1) adding malathion. ECL intensity of different modified electrodes: (a, a1) bare GCE; (b, b1) ENCF-67/GCE; (c, c1) Au/GCE; (d, d1) CQDs/GCE; (e, e1) ENCF-67@Au/GCE; (f, f1) ENCF-67/CQDs/GCE; (g, g1) Au/CQDs/GCE; (h, h1) ENCF-67/Au/CQDs/GCE; (i, i1) ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE; (j, j1) Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE. (C)The ECL intensity change (ΔI) of different electrode after malathion addition. (D) CV and (E) ECL intensity of the sensor with different concentrations of malathion: (a) 0 M (b) 1.0 × 10−7 M (c) 2.0 × 10−7 M (d) 3.0 × 10−7 M (e) 4.0 × 10−7 M (f) 5.0 × 10−7 M. (F) The possible ECL mechanisms of the Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE.
Effects of (A) pH of electrolyte solution; (B) the concentration of K2S2O8; (C) the volume of CQDs; (D) the concentration of ENCF-67@Au; (E) reaction time; (F) scanning rate.

(A) ECL responses of Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE with various malathion concentrations: a → f: 1.0 × 10−12, 1.0 × 10−11, 1.0 × 10−10, 1.0 × 10−9, 1.0 × 10−8, 1.0 × 10−7 M. (B) The linear calibration curve between ΔI and logarithm of malathion concentrations. (C) ECL signal in PBS (pH 7.0) under continuous cyclic voltammetry for 10 cycles. (D) Reproducibility test of the ECL sensor. (E) Long-term stability of the ECL sensor. (F) Selectivity of the ECL sensor.
Preparation of ENCF-67@Au(A) and CQDs (B); the fabrication process of the ECL sensors(C).
Performance comparison of different ECL sensors for the analysis of malathion.
Determination results of malathion in real samples.

head.ce:title: Solid-state electrochemiluminescence sensor of CQDs based on ZIFs electrospun carbon fiber for malathion detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Changchun Normal University, Changchun 130032, People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Changchun Normal University, Changchun 130032, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a novel and simple electrochemiluminescent sensor was constructed for the detection of malathion. ZIFs electrospun carbon fiber (ENCF-67) prepared by electrospinning combined with high temperature calcination adsorbed Au NPs to obtain ENCF-67@Au. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was obtained by pyrolysis of citric acid at high temperature. And the sensor of Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQD/GCE was constructed by sequentially modified CQDs, ENCF-67@Au and Nafion on the electrode surfaces. ENCF-67@Au was used as the fixation material of CQDs and accelerator of system because ENCF-67 had the advantage of large specific surface area and porosity. Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE obtained sensitive and stable electrochemical luminescence signal in PBS containing S2O8 2-. The sensor had good selectivity for malathion, which provided a simple and sensitive method for the detection of malathion in fruits and vegetables in real life and had potential applications.

In this paper, a novel and simple electrochemiluminescent sensor of CQDs based on ZIFs electrospun carbon fiber was constructed for the detection of malathion. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was obtained by pyrolysis of citric acid at high temperature. ZIFs electrospun carbon fiber (ENCF-67) prepared by electrospinning combined with high temperature calcination adsorbed Au NPs to obtain ENCF-67@Au. And the sensor of Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE was constructed by sequentially modified CQDs, ENCF-67@Au and Nafion on the electrode surfaces. ENCF-67@Au was used as the fixation material of CQDs and accelerator of system because ENCF-67 had the advantage of large specific surface area and porosity. Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE obtained sensitive and stable electrochemical luminescence signal in PBS containing S2O8 2-. The addition of malathion enhanced the ECL signal of the sensor with a linear increase in the range of 1.0 × 10-12 − 1.0 × 10-7 M and the limit of detection 3.3 × 10-13 M (S/N = 3). The sensor had good selectivity for malathion, which provided a simple and sensitive method for the detection of malathion in fruits and vegetables in real life and had potential applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are one of the most commonly used agricultural pesticides, most of which are highly toxic or toxic, and a few are low toxic. They are widely used to control plant diseases and insect pests [1,2]. Malathion is a kind of OPs with high efficiency and low toxicity, which can be widely used to kill pests in fruits, vegetables and plants [3]. However, malathion will remain in soil, water, fruits and vegetables, and eventually quickly goes into the body through the food [4,5], causing harm to human body. Therefore, the detection of malathion residues is crucial. The classical methods for the determination of malathion are mainly chromatographic analysis, such as gas chromatography (GC) [6], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [8], and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [9]. The results of malathion determination by chromatographic analysis have high accuracy and good reproducibility, but there are some limitations, such as expensive instruments and complicated sample pretreatment. Therefore, it is essential to find a method that can easily and accurately detect malathion. Recently, our research group has constructed a variety of malathion sensors using different nanomaterials, among which electrochemical luminescence sensor was the main one [10–12]. The measurement process was simple and convenient, and the measurement results were of high accuracy. It can be said that electrochemical luminescence method is a better choice for the determination of malathion.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) combines the advantages of traditional chemiluminescence (CL) and electrochemistry, has the advantages of high sensitivity, simplicity and stability, and is widely used in food detection and pesticide residues [13]. The commonly used electrochemical luminescence reagents are Ru(bpy)3 2+, luminol, quantum dots and so on. In recent years, quantum dots (QDs) as a new luminescent reagent have been widely concerned by researchers [14–16]. However, traditional ECL quantum dots have inherent toxicity, poor stability and low ECL strength at high electrochemical potential [17,18]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has the characteristics of green preparation and good biocompatibility, which can solve some defects of traditional quantum dots [19–22]. However, CQDs still have the defects of poor stability and low ECL signal. Some nanomaterials have been applied to carriers to address the deficiency of low ECL strength of CQDs. Zhang's team developed an ECL immune sensor based on Au/SiO2/CQDs core–shell nanoparticles for the detection of 8-OHdG. Using gold /SiO2 core–shell nanoparticles as substrate material, the ECL emission signal of CQDs was greatly improved [18]. Kamyabi's team designed a novel electrochemical luminescence probe using Cu/CQDs/g-C3N4NS ternary composite materials for accurate monitoring of diazinon. CQDs and copper sulfide nanostructures showed a good synergistic effect in signal improvement [23].

Electrospun carbon nanofibers (ENCF), with their high specific surface area and large porosity, have been used in a large number of applications in catalyst carriers, lithium battery electrode materials, and highly sensitive sensor devices in recent years [24–27]. Zeolite imidazole framework materials (ZIFs) are a branch of MOFs, whose structures have good porosity and thermochemical stability [28,29]. The electrospun carbon fibers containing ZIFs (ENCF-67) prepared by high temperature treatment ZIFs composite electrospinning fiber have better surface area, porosity and stability than the original backbone materials [30,31]. Based on this advantage, the ENCF-67 can be applied to the electrode surface as a carrier, thus improving the electrochemical performance of the sensor. In previous studies, we have used ENCF-67 as a carrier of Ru(bpy)3 2+ to construct a sensitive and stable solid-state ECL sensor, which can be applied to the determination of chlorpyrifos in actual samples [32]. Now we continue to give full play to the advantages of ENCF-67 and combine it with the ECL system of CQDs to solve the problems of poor stability and low luminescence signal of CQDs.

In this paper, a solid-state electrochemical luminescence sensor was constructed by fixing CQDs on the electrode surface with ENCF-67 as the carrier. CQDs, ENCF-67@Au and Nafion were successively modified on the electrode surface by drop coating method. With a large surface area and aperture, ENCF-67 could not only fix CQDs, but also fix Au NPs, and itself could promote electron transfer in the system. Under the synergistic action of the three, the solid-state electroluminescence sensor obtained satisfactory signals in PBS containing S2O8 2-. Malathion was able to enhance the ECL value of the system, and the enhancement value showed regular changed within a certain concentration range. Therefore, an ECL sensor was constructed for the determination of malathion. In this study, ENCF-67 was combined with CQDs electrochemical luminescence system for the first time, which solved the problems of low signal and poor stability of CQDs electrochemical luminescence system.

Malathion, Polyacrylonitrile (PAN), 2-Methylimidazole (2-MI), citric acid (CA), Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2) and chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) were all provided by Aladdin Chemistry Co., Phosphate buffer solution was prepared using 0.1 M Na2HPO4, 0.1 M NaH2PO4 and 0.1 M NaOH. All other chemical reagents are analysis of pure.

The MPI-B ECL analyzer for electrochemiluminescence testing was provided from Xi’an Remax Electronic Science & Technology Co. Ltd. (Xi’an, China). CHI 660A electrochemical workstation for determination of cyclic voltammetry (CV) was from Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China). Electrostatic spinning equipment (HD-1311) for preparing electrospinning fibers was purchased from Beijing Yongkang Leye Technology Development Co., Ltd. Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM Thermo Fisher Scientific Apreo 2S+ ). The morphologies of the CQDs was characterized by the transmission electronmicroscope (JEM 2100F), which from JEOL (Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD Bruker Apex CCD diffract meter, Germany) and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS OXFORDU ltim Max 65). The experiments were carried out using a conventional three-electrode system (reference electrode: Ag/AgCl, auxiliary electrode: platinum electrode, working electrode: glassy carbon electrode).
ZIF-67 was prepared with slight improvements according to reference [33], and the details were in the supplementary materials.
We continued to use ENCF-67, which had good performance, as the sensor carrier, the preparation process was consistent with the reference [32] and listed in the supplementary materials.

Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) were prepared by reducing chloroauric acid with sodium citrate as a reducing agent with reference to those prepared by previous reports [34] and the detailed preparation process was listed in the supplementary materials.

20 mg of ENCF-67 was immersed in 5 mL of Au NPs solution for 48 h, then centrifuged, the upper liquid was discarded, and the lower solid was dried and named ENCF-67@Au. 1 mg of ENCF-67@Au was added 10 mL of water and sonicated for 48 h to obtain ENCF-67@Au dispersion, and put it in 4 ℃ refrigerator for subsequent use.

CQDs was prepared according to the reference [35]. 4 g of citric acid (CA) was heated directly at 200 °C in a three-necked flask. CA liquefied during heating, and its color changed from colorless to yellow and finally to orange, indicating the successful synthesis of CQDs. The prepared CQDs were then diluted with 25 mL of water and placed in the refrigerator for use in subsequent experimental procedures.

Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE was modified according to Scheme 1 . Firstly, the GCE was polished with Al2O3 powder and blown dry with nitrogen gas (N2). 5 μL of CQDs were modified on the clean electrode surface, and CQDs/GCE were obtained after drying by infrared light. 5 μL of 0.1 mg mL−1 ENCF-67@Au solution was immobilized on the surface of CQDs/GCE. After infrared drying, ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE was obtained. Finally, 5 μL of 0.05 % Nafion solution was modified onto the electrode and dried to obtain Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE.

Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE was detected in PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8. The ECL parameters were set to a voltage range between −2.0–0.0 V and a scan rate of 100 mV/s. The voltage of photomultiplier tube was placed at 800 V.

The SEM results of ENCF-67 and Au NPs were shown in supplementary materials (Fig. S1). According to the Fig. S1A, after high temperature calcination at 800 ℃, some small pores appeared on the surface of ENCF-67, which may be caused by the collapse of the framework of ZIF-67 NFs after high temperature calcination. From the Fig. S1B, Au NPs was spherical with an average particle size of about 30 nm, which was consistent with the reference [36]. CQDs were characterized by TEM, FT-IR, UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy (FL). The TEM results of CQDs (Fig. 1 A) showed that the synthesized CQDs were more uniformly distributed and all were spherical. The particle size was about 4 nm. It can be seen from Fig. 1B that the characteristic peaks in the infrared spectrum of CQDs correspond to those in the reference [37]. The UV absorption spectra of the synthesized CQDs (Fig. 1C) were compared with the references, and the absorption peaks were found to be in good agreement with the reference [38]. The FL emission spectrum of CQDs was shown in Fig. 1D, and the fluorescence emission peak of CQDs was located at 472 nm. The above results were consistent with previous work [39]. The insert of Fig. 1D showed that the synthetic CQDs solution was yellow under natural light (left) and its color changed to blue fluorescence when irradiated under 365 nm UV light (right). In conclusion, CQDs was successfully prepared. Fig. 1E showed the XRD test results of ENCF-67 and ENCF-67@Au. The XRD of ENCF-67 showed diffraction peaks at 26°, 44°, 51° and 76°, which was consistent with the XRD results of ENCF-67 in previous studies [32]. The XRD results of ENCF-67@Au showed diffraction peaks at 38°, 44°, 64°, and 77°, which basically matched with the (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystallographic planes of gold and indicating that Au NPs were successfully adsorbed by ENCF-67. SEM results showed that tiny particles could be seen on the surface of ENCF-67, indicating that Au NPs may be adsorbed on the surface of carbon fiber, while EDS test detected C, N, O, Co and Au elements, which just verified this result (Fig. 1F). The results of XRD, SEM and EDS showed that the prepared materials adsorbed gold nanoparticles successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL property of different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was adopted to characterize the preparation process of this sensor (Fig. 2 A). Each modification phase of the sensor was determined by EIS in 0.1 M [Fe(CN)6]3− /4− solution. It can be seen that the electron transfer resistance (Ret) of the bare GCE was almost a straight line (curve a). After modifying CQDs onto the GCE surface, the Ret of CQDs/GCE became larger (curve b), which may be caused by a film was formed on the surface of the electrode, blocking the transmission of electrons. It was indicating that CQDs had been successfully modified onto the electrode surface. Subsequent modification of ENCF-67@Au onto the surface of CQDs/GCE, Ret further increased (curve c). This may be because although ENCF-67 could promote electron transfer, it may hinder electron transfer due to its relatively thickness on the electrode surface, thus increased the resistance. Finally, Nafion modified to ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE was constructed as Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE, and the Ret increased significantly (curve d), which was due to the formation of a film on the electrode surface by Nafion, hindering the electron transfer of system. With the modification of various materials, the Ret value of the electrode increased continuously. The Ret values of each layer were 156 (curve a), 213 (curve b), 1053 (curve c), 1147 Ω (curve d), respectively. The results indicated that Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE had been successfully prepared. The preparation of the sensor was characterized using cyclic voltammetry and the results were showed in the supplementary material. The results of CV (Fig. S2) were consistent with the results of EIS and prove the success of sensor preparation.

Fig. 2B showed the ECL signals of bare GCE, ENCF-67/GCE, Au/GCE, CQDs/GCE, ENCF-67@Au/GCE, ENCF-67/CQDs/GCE, Au/CQDs/GCE, ENCF-67/Au/CQDs/GCE, ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE, Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE. Bare GCE had a weak ECL value of 680 a.u. (curve a), which may be due to the weak electrical signal generated by the reduction reaction of S2O8 2-. The ECL intensity of ENCF-67/GCE and Au/GCE was enhanced after the surface of the bare electrode was modified with ENCF-67 and Au NPs respectively. And the ECL signal of ENCF-67/GCE and Au/ GCE was 1357 a.u. and 3621 a.u. respectively. This may be due to the porosity of ENCF-67 promoting electron transfer, while Au NPs can promote the electron transfer of S2O8 2- reduction and system. However, the luminescence signal of Au/GCE was unstable, so ENCF-67 was used to host Au NPs. When ENCF-67@Au was modified on bare GCE, the ECL intensity increased to 4859 a.u. (curve e), and the signal was relatively stable. The prepared CQDs were modified on the bare electrode, CQDs participated in the luminescence reaction as a luminescent reagent, and the ECL signal value of CQDs/GCE was 4326 a.u (curve d). So as to study the synergistic effect of ENCF-67@Au and CQDs, the ECL intensity of ENCF-67/CQDs/GCE (curve f), Au/CQDs/GCE (curve g), ENCF-67/Au/CQDs/GCE (curve h), ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE (curve i) were all compared experimentally. ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE obtained the largest and more stable ECL signal, reaching 9762 a.u.. This phenomenon may be attributed to the large specific surface area and porosity of ENCF-67, which could fix Au NPs and CQDs on the electrode surface. ENCF-67@Au and CQDs had good synergistic effect. In order to obtain a more stable ECL system, a further layer of Nafion was modified on the electrode. Nafion generated a film in the outermost layer, which hindered electron transport, and the ECL signal decreased slightly (curve j).

Curves a1, b1, c1, d1, e1, f1, g1, h1, i1, j1 showed the ECL signals of different modified electrodes with malathion. Obviously, the addition of malathion increased the ECL strength of the above electrodes. Fig. 2C demonstrated the elevation values (ΔI) of different modified electrodes. From the figure, ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE had the largest ΔI, followed by Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE. Considering the stability of the sensor, Nafion/ENCF-67@Au/CQDs/GCE was used as the working electrode in subsequent tests.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and ECL testing of the assembled electrodes in PBS solutions containing different concentrations of malathion (Fig. 2D and Fig. 2E). The peak current and ECL intensity were enhanced regularly with increasing concentration of malathion. And the increase value of ECL signal was more obvious.

The possible ECL mechanisms were described in Fig. 2F. Firstly, an electron transfer reaction between the reduced species formed in the CQDs and the oxide (SO4 •-) of the co-reactant to generate the ECL signal. And the possible mechanism could be summarized as follows: CQDs, which were reduced to CQDs•- on the negative potential polarization, while the co-reactant S2O8 2- was reduced to the strong oxidant SO4 •- [40]. The generation of luminescent excited state CQDs* was due to SO4 •- could react with the negatively charged CQDs•- by electron transfer. The excited state CQDs* finally returned to the ground state CQDs and emitted energy and occurred the ECL phenomenon (equation 1–5). The introduction of ENCF-67@Au can be used as an accelerator of the system. First of all, Au NPs had good conductivity, which could promote the electron transfer of S2O8 2- on the electrode surface more quickly, but also effectively catalyzed the redox reaction of S2O8 2- to produce more SO4 •-. Secondly ENCF-67 could not only promote electron transfer but also adsorb more Au NPs because of its large specific surface area and porosity. Finally, the synergistic effect of ENCF-67 and Au increased the system ECL signal [41]. After malathion was added to the system, malathion may be hydrolyzed under negative voltage, and the hydrolyzed products may interact with S2O8 2-, and the generated products react with the free radicals generated by CQDs to generate more free radicals, thus promoting the luminous intensity of the system [23].
(1)
(2)
(3)
(4)
(5)

In order to obtain higher sensitivity, the experimental conditions such as pH, the concentrations of CQDs, K2S2O8 and ENCF-67@Au were all investigated, the results were showed in Fig. 3 . Meanwhile, the reaction time and scanning speed were optimized by fixing the above conditions.
Fig. 3A showed the influence of pH. The value of ΔI change tended to increase when the pH value was from 6.0 to 7.0, and then ΔI gradually decreased when the pH value was greater than 7.0.

Fig. 3B showed the influence of the concentration of K2S2O8 on ΔI in the range of 0.05–0.15 M. The concentration of K2S2O8 at 0.1 M, ΔI at the maximum. This may be because when the concentration was too small, the S2O8 2- did not react adequately with CQDs and did not give a good ECL signal. Therefore, 0.1 M was chosen as the optimal value for the concentration of K2S2O8.

The modification amount of CQDs was an important factor affecting the ECL intensity. During the study, the concentration of CQDs was fixed and the modification volume was changed. Fig. 3C showed the effect of the modified volume of CQDs on the ECL signal of the system. The results showed that ΔI firstly tended to increase with the increase of the modified volume of CQDs, and reached the maximum value when the volume of CQDs was 5 μL. And then ΔI gradually decreased when the volume was larger than 5 μL subsequently. The reason for the decrease of ΔI may be that the larger the volume of the modified CQDs on the electrode surface, the greater the thickness on the electrode surface, which hindered the transfer of electrons.

In addition, the amount of ENCF-67@Au modification was also an important factor affecting the sensor ECL signal (Fig. 3D). In the study, the modification volume of ENCF-67@Au was fixed, the concentration of ENCF-67@Au was changed, and the effect of ENCF-67@Au modification on ECL signal of the system was investigated. The results showed that ΔI increased first and then decreased. Therefore, the concentration of ENCF-67@Au in subsequent experiments was 0.1 mg mL−1.

The results of the effect of reaction time and scanning speed on the ECL signal were presented in Fig. 3E and Fig. 3F, which was finally confirmed after optimization of the experimental conditions to a reaction time of 5 min and a scanning speed of 0.1 Vs−1 for CV.

Malathion was measured under optimal conditions. Fig. 4 A demonstrated the ECL values for different concentrations of malathion, which increased with increasing concentration, and Fig. 4B demonstrated the linear relationship between the concentration of malathion and ECL. The results showed that ΔI had a good linear relationship with the logarithm of malathion concentration, with a linear range between 1.0 × 10−12 − 1.0 × 10−7 M. The linear equation of the sensor was ΔI = 11766.8 + 876.9lgCmalathion , with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.994 and a detection limit of 3.3 × 10−13 M (S/N = 3). Table 1 showed the results of a comparison of the modified sensor with ECL sensors constructed by other methods. Firstly, the sensor constructed was simple in preparation process and material, and did not use acetylcholinesterase or aptamer. Secondly, compared with other ECL sensors, the sensor constructed by this method had a wide linear range and low detection limit.

To investigate the stability of the sensor, 10 consecutive measurements were made in PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8 and 1.0 × 10−12 M malathion in the range of −2.0 ∼ 0.0 V (Fig. 4C). The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 1.92 %. Satisfactory results for sensor stability. At the same time, we also tested the long-term stability of the sensor, and the results were shown in Fig. 4D. The 12 simultaneously modified working electrodes were placed in a refrigerator at 4℃, and two electrodes were taken out every two days to test the ECL strength. The ECL test result of the electrode on day 10 was 90.7 % of the test result on day 0. Within 10 days, the ECL signal of this sensor did not vary much, within 10 %, and the results were satisfactory.

In addition, reproducibility was investigated with five electrodes (Fig. 4E). Five electrodes were simultaneously modified using the same method to test malathion at a concentration of 1.0 × 10−12 M. The experimental showed that the RSD = 2.37 %, indicating that the sensor had good repeatability.

Procymidone, chlorpyrifos, indoxacarb, permethrin, dimethoate, sulfotep, methidathion, and fenpropathrin were added to the system to test the selectivity of the constructed sensor. The results were shown in Fig. 4F. When interfering substances with concentrations of 100 times malathion were added, the ΔI value was smaller than malathion. Thus, it could be demonstrated that the constructed sensor had good selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of malathion in fruit and vegetable samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the feasibility of the sensor, apples, celery, and Chinese cabbage were purchased as test samples at a local supermarket. The apples, celery and cabbage were pretreated and added with different concentrations of malathion for recovery experiments, details of pretreatment could be found in supplementary materials. Table 2 showed the results of the spiked analysis of malathion in the three samples. The recoveries of malathion in the three samples ranged from 98.2 % to 106. 3 % with the RSD less than 4.2 %, the results showed that the sensor could be used to detect malathion. Gas chromatography (GC) and ECL were used for comparison. The above results showed that the method was reliable for the detection of malathion in real samples. It had a fast, sensitive detection capability.

In this work, a simple and novel ECL sensor based on ENCF-67@Au and CQDs was constructed to detect malathion. The prepared carbon nanofibers containing ZIF-67 had large specific surface area and porosity, which could be used as the carrier of Au NPs and CQDs and the promoter of electron transfer in the system. As a kind of green electrochemiluminescence reagent, CQDs had obvious luminescence in K2S2O8, and Au NPs was a co-reaction promoter of CQDs. The synergistic effect of these three reagents significantly enhanced the sensitivity of ECL sensor. Compared with other ECL sensors, the sensor prepared in this study had the advantages of simple preparation process, simple reagent, low detection limit, wide linear range and so on. In this study, electrospun carbon nanofibers and CQDs electrochemiluminescence system were combined for the first time, which provided a new pathway for carbon fiber in ECL system. However, this experiment also had the disadvantage of insufficient interference experimental research, which would be optimized in the subsequent experiments.

Xiangyu Shan: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Juan Lu: Methodology, Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Qian Wu: Methodology, Investigation, Software, Formal analysis. Chao Li: Formal analysis, Supervision. Huiling Li: Data curation, Supervision. Shuning Yang: Data curation, Supervision. Li Tian: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Jilin Education Foundation (JJKH20230905KJ).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109496.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
